text: Neural machine translation (NMT) represents a paradigm shift in statistical translation, utilizing end-to-end neural networks to map source sequences to target languages. Typically, these architectures rely on an encoder-decoder framework: the encoder compresses a variable-length input sentence into a fixed-size vector representation, which the decoder then uses to generate a translation. This paper investigates the nuanced properties of NMT by evaluating two distinct architectures: the traditional RNN Encoder-Decoder and a novel gated recursive convolutional neural network. Our analysis reveals that while NMT systems excel at translating short sentences with familiar vocabulary, their performance deteriorates significantly as sentence length increases or when encountering out-of-vocabulary words. Additionally, our findings demonstrate that the proposed gated recursive convolutional network is capable of autonomously capturing the underlying grammatical structure of sentences during the learning process.
